questions.q: State the basic rule for the force between magnetic poles.
options: Like poles attract; unlike poles repel
Like poles repel; unlike poles attract
All poles attract each other
Poles exert no force on each other
questions.explanation: Like magnetic poles (two norths or two souths) repel, while unlike poles (a north and a south) attract — the same pattern as electric charges. Unlike charges, however, magnetic poles always come in north-south pairs.

questions.q: Explain why it is impossible to obtain a single isolated magnetic pole.
options: Poles are too small to separate
Poles always come in north-south pairs; cutting a magnet just makes two smaller complete magnets
A single pole would have infinite energy
Only electric charges can be isolated because they are stronger
questions.explanation: Magnetic poles always exist in north-south pairs. Cutting a magnet in an attempt to separate them simply produces two smaller complete magnets, each with its own north and south, no matter how finely you divide it. A lone pole (a monopole) has never been found — unlike electric charge, which can be isolated.

questions.q: Explain, in terms of domains, how a piece of iron becomes magnetised.
options: Its atoms are destroyed and rebuilt
Its randomly oriented domains align with an external field, so their fields add to give a net magnetism
It gains extra electrons from the magnet
Its temperature rises until it melts
questions.explanation: A ferromagnetic material contains tiny domains, each a small magnet. Unmagnetised, the domains point randomly and their fields cancel. In an external field the domains rotate to align, so their fields add together, giving a strong net magnetisation. Naming the random-to-aligned change is the key mark.

questions.q: Identify the direction of magnetic field lines outside a bar magnet.
options: From south pole to north pole
From north pole to south pole
In closed circles around the magnet's centre
Always pointing straight up
questions.explanation: Outside a magnet, field lines run from the north pole to the south pole — the direction the north end of a small compass points. The lines are closer together where the field is stronger (near the poles) and never cross. (Inside the magnet they continue from S to N to form closed loops.)

questions.q: Explain why the Earth's geographic North Pole is actually near a magnetic south pole.
options: Because the Earth has no magnetic field
Because a compass's north-seeking end is attracted there, and unlike poles attract
Because the north and south poles are the same
Because the Earth spins eastward
questions.explanation: The north (north-seeking) end of a compass needle points toward geographic north, and since unlike poles attract, the magnetic pole there must be a south pole. The Earth behaves like a giant bar magnet, and its field also deflects solar particles to create the auroras.

questions.q: Identify the shape of the magnetic field around a long straight current-carrying wire.
options: A bar-magnet pattern with distinct poles
Concentric circles centred on the wire, perpendicular to it
Straight lines parallel to the wire
No field is produced
questions.explanation: A current-carrying wire produces concentric circular field lines centred on the wire, lying in planes perpendicular to it, with strength falling off with distance (B = μ₀I/2πr). The direction is given by the right-hand grip rule. A solenoid (a coil), by contrast, gives a bar-magnet pattern — don't confuse the two.

questions.q: A vertical wire carries conventional current straight upward. Using the right-hand grip rule, determine the field direction as viewed from above.
options: Clockwise
Anticlockwise
Straight up the wire
Radially outward
questions.explanation: Point the right thumb up (along the current); the fingers curl anticlockwise as seen from above. So the field circulates anticlockwise. Using the left hand, or pointing the thumb down, gives the wrong (clockwise) answer — it must be the right hand, thumb along the conventional current.

questions.q: A solenoid of 200 turns and length 0.5 m carries 3 A (μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷). Calculate the field inside it.
questions.explanation: B = μ₀NI/L = (4π×10⁻⁷ × 200 × 3)/0.5 ≈ 7.54×10⁻⁴ / 0.5 ≈ 1.5×10⁻³ T (1.5 mT). The field grows with more current, more turns, or a shorter length. Forgetting to divide by the length is the common error.

questions.q: Identify two advantages an electromagnet has over a permanent magnet.
options: It is always magnetic and cannot be switched off
It can be switched on and off, and its strength varied (or poles reversed) by changing the current
It needs no electric current to work
It can isolate a single magnetic pole
questions.explanation: An electromagnet (a solenoid with a ferromagnetic core) is magnetic only while current flows, so it can be switched on/off, its strength varied by changing the current or turns, and its poles reversed by reversing the current. The iron core's domains align with the coil's field to greatly strengthen it.

questions.q: Recommend the type of magnetic material best suited for the core of a switchable electromagnet.
options: Magnetically hard material (e.g. steel), because it keeps its magnetism
Magnetically soft material (e.g. pure iron), because it magnetises and demagnetises easily
A non-magnetic material like copper
Any material, since the core makes no difference
questions.explanation: A magnetically soft material like pure iron magnetises strongly when the current is on but loses its magnetism the instant the current is switched off — exactly what a switchable electromagnet needs. Magnetically hard materials (steel) keep their magnetism, so they are used for permanent magnets instead.
